content:
# Luke Chapter 8 — Disagreement Log

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Translations compared:** EMET (Literal), LOGOS (Formal), PNEUMA (Dynamic)
**Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 95-97

---

## Summary

Luke 8 contains 56 verses spanning the Parable of the Sower (vv. 1-15), lamp and family sayings (vv. 16-21), the stilling of the storm (vv. 22-25), the Gerasene demoniac (vv. 26-39), Jairus's daughter and the woman with the flow of blood (vv. 40-56). Significant disagreements arose around the rendering of *sozo* ("save/heal"), the treatment of the Semitic idiom in v. 28, the *monogenes* question in v. 42, and the preservation of Sinaiticus's historical present tense in v. 49.

## Disagreement Table

| Verse | Issue | EMET (Literal) | LOGOS (Formal) | PNEUMA (Dynamic) | SOPHIA Decision | Rationale |
|-------|-------|----------------|----------------|-------------------|-----------------|-----------|
| 8:4 | Crowd description | "those from each city journeying to him" | "those from every city were coming to him" | "people streaming to him from town after town" | "people from every city coming to him" | Blends LOGOS accuracy with PNEUMA naturalness |
| 8:5 | Sower rendering | "The one sowing" | "The sower" | "A farmer" | "The sower" | Greek *ho speiron* is participial noun; "sower" is standard; "farmer" too broad |
| 8:8 | Ears formula | "The one having ears to hear, let him hear" | "He who has ears to hear, let him hear" | "Whoever has ears to hear, let them hear!" | "He who has ears to hear, let him hear" | Preserves Greek masculine singular; formal register appropriate for dominical saying |
| 8:14 | Fruit completion | "bring fruit to completion" | "bring fruit to maturity" | "produce mature fruit" | "bring fruit to maturity" | "Maturity" captures agricultural metaphor of *telesphoreo* better than abstract "completion" |
| 8:15 | Heart adjectives | "noble and good" | "noble and good" | "honest and good" | "noble and good" | 2-to-1; *kalos* = noble/beautiful/fine, not merely "honest" |
| 8:18 | Opening imperative | "See then" | "Take heed therefore" | "So pay attention" | "Take heed therefore" | Formal register; preserves admonitory force of *blepete oun* |
| 8:20 | "wanting" vs "wishing" | "wanting to see you" | "wishing to see you" | "wanting to see you" | "wanting to see you" | 2-to-1; more natural in modern English |
| 8:23 | Storm description | "a storm of wind" | "a violent windstorm" | "a fierce windstorm" | "a violent windstorm" | LOGOS captures the compound *lailaps anemou* with appropriate force |
| 8:24 | Water description | "the raging of the water" | "the surge of the water" | "the raging waves" | "the surging of the water" | *kludon* = surging/billowing of water; noun form preserves Greek structure |
| 8:25 | Fear description | "having been afraid, they marveled" | "being afraid, they marveled" | "Shaken and amazed" | "being afraid, they marveled" | Preserves two-stage Greek response (fear then wonder); PNEUMA compresses |
| 8:28 | Semitic idiom *ti emoi kai soi* | "What is there to me and to you" | "What have I to do with you" | "What do you want with me" | "What have I to do with you" | Traditional English rendering of Semitic idiom; preserves foreignness and force |
| 8:29 | Bonds terminology | "chains and fetters" | "chains and shackles" | "chains and shackles" | "chains and shackles" | 2-to-1; "shackles" more immediately clear than archaic "fetters" |
| 8:32 | Animal term | "pigs" | "swine" | "pigs" | "pigs" | 2-to-1; "swine" is archaic; Greek *choiroi* is the common word |
| 8:32 | Location | "on the mountain" | "on the mountain" | "on the hillside" | "on the mountain" | 2-to-1; Sinaiticus reads *en to orei*; PNEUMA's "hillside" is interpretive |
| 8:33 | Herd motion | "rushed down the steep bank" | "rushed down the steep bank" | "stampeded down the steep bank" | "rushed down the steep bank" | 2-to-1; *hormesen* = rushed/charged; "stampeded" adds interpretive color |
| 8:36 | *esothe* rendering | "was saved" | "had been saved" | "had been made well" | "had been saved" | *sozo* = save/heal; "saved" preserves dual meaning; PNEUMA's "made well" reduces |
| 8:37 | Departure verb | "go away" | "depart" | "leave" | "depart" | "Depart" maintains formal register appropriate to the solemnity of communal rejection |
| 8:39 | Reported content | "what things" | "what great things" | "everything" | "what great things" | LOGOS follows the *hosa* ("how great/many things") reading faithfully |
| 8:42 | *monogenes* | "only-born" | "only-begotten" | "only" | "only daughter" | Modern scholarship favors "only/unique" for *monogenes*; compound "-begotten" is disputed |
| 8:46 | *egnon* rendering | "I knew" | "I perceived" | "I felt" | "I perceived" | "Perceived" captures the aorist moment of awareness; "knew" implies ongoing state; "felt" too subjective |
| 8:48 | *sesoken* rendering | "has saved you" | "has saved you" | "has made you well" | "has saved you" | 2-to-1; preserves full semantic range of *sozo*; consistent with 7:50 |
| 8:49 | Verb tense of *erchetai* | "comes" (historical present) | "comes" (historical present) | "came" (past tense) | "comes" (historical present) | 2-to-1; preserves Sinaiticus's vivid historical present at this dramatic turning point |
| 8:50 | *sothesetai* rendering | "she shall be saved" | "she shall be saved" | "she will be made well" | "she shall be saved" | 2-to-1; consistency with *sozo* throughout; "shall" preserves formal tone |
| 8:52 | Death/sleep statement | "she did not die, but she sleeps" | "she has not died but is sleeping" | "She is not dead -- she is sleeping" | "she has not died but is sleeping" | LOGOS best preserves Greek tenses (perfect + present) |
| 8:54 | Command to child | "Child, arise" | "Child, arise" | "Child, get up!" | "Child, arise" | 2-to-1; "arise" preserves resurrection language and solemnity; consistent with 7:14 |
| 8:55 | Rising description | "rose up immediately" | "rose up at once" | "stood up at once" | "rose up at once" | *aneste* = "rose up" (not merely "stood up"); "at once" preferred over "immediately" for variety |
| 8:56 | Parental response | "were astonished" | "were amazed" | "were astonished" | "were amazed" | *exestesan* = "were beside themselves"; "amazed" is precise; avoids overuse of "astonished" |

## Thematic Disagreement: The *sozo* Question

The most significant recurring disagreement in this chapter concerns the Greek verb *sozo* and its cognates, which appear in verses 36, 48, 50, and arguably 12. EMET and LOGOS consistently render these as "saved," while PNEUMA renders them as "made well" (vv. 48, 50) or "made well" (v. 36). SOPHIA sides with EMET and LOGOS on this point for three reasons:

1. **Lexical fidelity:** The Greek *sozo* genuinely encompasses both physical healing and spiritual salvation. Reducing it to "made well" strips the theological dimension.
2. **Internal consistency:** Luke uses the same verb when Jesus says "your faith has saved you" to the sinful woman in 7:50 and to the healed woman in 8:48. The verbal echo is deliberate and should be preserved.
3. **Authorial intent:** Luke, more than any other evangelist, exploits the double meaning of *sozo*. The reader should encounter the same word and wrestle with its range, as the original audience did.

## Textual Notes from Sinaiticus

- **8:3** — Sinaiticus reads ΑΥΤΟΙΣ ("to them," plural), not αυτω ("to him"). The women ministered to Jesus and the twelve collectively.
- **8:26, 37** — Sinaiticus consistently reads ΓΕΡΑΣΗΝΩΝ ("Gerasenes"), not Gadarenes or Gergesenes.
- **8:32** — Sinaiticus reads ΕΝ ΤΩ ΟΡΕΙ ("on the mountain"), not "on the hillside."
- **8:45** — Sinaiticus attributes the response to ΠΕΤΡΟΣ (Peter) alone, without "and those with him."
- **8:46** — Sinaiticus reads ΕΓΝΩΝ (aorist "I perceived") without the emphatic εγω γαρ of the Byzantine tradition.
- **8:49** — Sinaiticus preserves the historical present ΕΡΧΕΤΑΙ ("comes"), creating vivid immediacy.
- **8:51** — Sinaiticus reads "Peter and John and James" (Lukan order), placing John before James.

---

*Compiled by SOPHIA from the council of EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA.*
